threats.title: Authentication Bypass
threats.description: Attacker may bypass authentication in web application
threats.attack_vector: Credential stuffing, session hijacking, token theft
threats.impact: Unauthorized access to user accounts and data

mitigations.title: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication
mitigations.description: Add a second factor to verify user identity beyond passwords
implementation_steps: Choose MFA provider (Okta, Auth0, Duo, etc.)
Integrate MFA SDK into authentication flow

test_cases.title: Verify MFA enrollment flow
test_cases.description: Test that users can successfully enroll in MFA
steps: Verify second factor is required on next login
test_cases.expected_result: User cannot log in with just password after MFA enrollment

test_cases.title: Test MFA bypass attempts
test_cases.description: Attempt to bypass MFA using common techniques
steps: Try direct API access without MFA

recommendations: Regular security assessments and penetration testing
Security awareness training for development team

dfd_mermaid:
flowchart TD
    subgraph External[External Entities]
        User["👤 User"]
    end
    subgraph System[web application]
        C0["React Frontend"]
        C1["Node.js Backend"]
        C2["PostgreSQL Database"]
    end
    subgraph Data[Data Stores]
        DB[("Database")]
    end

    User --> C0
    C0 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> DB

conversation_history.content: I am building a web application using React frontend and Node.js backend with PostgreSQL database. It handles user authentication and stores customer payment information.

{"analysis": "Okay, you're building a web application with a React frontend, Node.js backend, and PostgreSQL database. This is a common architecture and a good starting point. The application handles user authentication and stores customer payment information, which immediately flags it as a high-risk area from a security perspective.  Storing payment information requires careful consideration of PCI DSS compliance, which will heavily influence the threat model.", "completeness_score": 0.3, "missing_info": ["Specific authentication mechanism (e.g., OAuth 2.0, JWT, traditional username/password)", "Details on the payment processing flow (e.g., integration with a payment gateway like Stripe or PayPal)", "Level of PCI DSS compliance requirements (e.g., Store, E-commerce, Merchant)", "Data volume and sensitivity of the payment data", "Details on the Node.js backend architecture (e.g., microservices, monolithic)", "Security measures currently planned or implemented (e.g., input validation, encryption)"], "follow_up_questions": ["Could you elaborate on the authentication mechanism you're planning to use? Specifically, are you using a standard protocol like OAuth 2.0 or JWT, or a custom solution?", "What payment gateway are you integrating with, if any? (e.g., Stripe, PayPal, Braintree)", "What is your intended level of PCI DSS compliance? (e.g., Do you anticipate storing full card data, or are you using tokenization?) "], "ready_for_threat_model": false, "world_model": {"system_type": "web application", "components": ["React Frontend", "Node.js Backend", "PostgreSQL Database"], "data_types": ["User Credentials", "Customer Payment Information (potentially including card data)"], "auth_method": "Unknown (requires further clarification)", "network_exposure": "Unknown (requires further clarification)", "compliance": [], "ai_involved": false}}

We use JWT for authentication with bcrypt password hashing. We integrate with Stripe for payments using tokenization so we never store full card data. The app is exposed to the public internet with HTTPS. We need to be GDPR and PCI-DSS compliant.

{"analysis": "Thank you for the additional information. You've confirmed JWT authentication with bcrypt, integration with Stripe for tokenized payments, public internet exposure via HTTPS, and a need for GDPR and PCI-DSS compliance. This significantly refines our understanding. The use of tokenization is a positive step for PCI-DSS compliance, but we still need to delve deeper into the specifics of your implementation and security controls. ", "completeness_score": 0.7, "missing_info": ["Details on the specific security controls you're implementing beyond tokenization (e.g., input validation, output encoding, rate limiting)", "Specific GDPR compliance measures being taken (e.g., data minimization, consent management)", "Network architecture details (e.g., firewall rules, intrusion detection system)", "Deployment environment details (e.g., cloud provider, infrastructure as code)"], "follow_up_questions": ["Can you describe the specific input validation and output encoding techniques you're using to protect against common web vulnerabilities like XSS and SQL injection?", "What mechanisms are in place to manage user consent and data subject rights under GDPR?", "Could you outline your network security posture, including your firewall configuration and any intrusion detection/prevention systems you're utilizing?"], "ready_for_threat_model": false, "world_model": {"system_type": "web application", "components": ["React Frontend", "Node.js Backend", "PostgreSQL Database"], "data_types": ["User Credentials", "Customer Payment Information (tokenized)"], "auth_method": "JWT with bcrypt", "network_exposure": "public internet (HTTPS)", "compliance": ["GDPR", "PCI-DSS"], "ai_involved": false}}

We use parameterized queries for SQL injection prevention and React auto-escapes for XSS. We have rate limiting on the API. For GDPR we have consent management and data deletion capabilities. We deploy on AWS with CloudFront CDN, WAF, and are behind an AWS ALB. We use AWS Secrets Manager for secrets.

{"analysis": "Okay, this provides a much more detailed picture. You've implemented several key security controls, including parameterized queries, React auto-escaping, rate limiting, a robust consent management system for GDPR, and a sophisticated deployment environment leveraging AWS services like CloudFront, WAF, ALB, and Secrets Manager. The use of Secrets Manager is particularly important for securing your credentials. This significantly improves our understanding of the system's security posture. However, we still need to validate the effectiveness of these controls and identify potential attack vectors that might bypass them.", "completeness_score": 0.9, "missing_info": ["Detailed configuration of the WAF (e.g., rule sets, custom rules)", "Specific monitoring and logging practices (e.g., SIEM integration)", "Testing and validation procedures for security controls", "Details on the data retention policy"], "follow_up_questions": ["Could you describe the specific WAF rule sets you're using and whether you've implemented any custom rules to address specific threats?", "What logging and monitoring solutions are in place to detect and respond to security incidents?", "What is your data retention policy for customer payment information?"], "ready_for_threat_model": true, "world_model": {"system_type": "web application", "components": ["React Frontend", "Node.js Backend", "PostgreSQL Database"], "data_types": ["User Credentials", "Customer Payment Information (tokenized)"], "auth_method": "JWT with bcrypt", "network_exposure": "public internet (HTTPS)", "compliance": ["GDPR", "PCI-DSS"], "ai_involved": false, "infrastructure": "AWS (CloudFront, WAF, ALB, Secrets Manager)"}}
